STATS 326/786 — Lab 03 Model Answer

Week 3 Tutorial

Problem 1 — Australian Retail Turnover

Setup — Selecting a Time Series

We filter to a single series: Queensland — Takeaway food services. Any valid series is acceptable; the commentary below is specific to this choice.

d <- aus_retail %>%
  filter(State    == "Queensland",
         Industry == "Takeaway food services")

Part 1 — Time Plot

Select one time series, plot it, and comment on what you observe.

Solution

d %>%
  autoplot(Turnover) +
  labs(title = "Queensland: Takeaway Food Services",
       y = "Turnover ($M AUD)", x = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

Explanation

Feature Observation
Trend Strong positive trend; approximately linear but with slight acceleration post-2000
Seasonality Regular annual peaks and troughs — appears multiplicative because the amplitude grows with the level
Cycles Possible business-cycle fluctuation of ~5–8 years, more visible from 1995 onward
Outliers / structural breaks No obvious single outliers; a visible dip around 2020 (COVID-19 impact)

Additive vs multiplicative seasonality: When the size of seasonal swings grows proportionally with the level of the series, the seasonality is multiplicative. This matters for choosing an appropriate decomposition method (e.g., multiplicative STL or log-transformation before fitting an additive model).

Part 2 — Seasonal & Subseries Plots

Create a seasonal plot and a seasonal subseries plot. Comment on any interesting patterns.

Solution

d %>% gg_season(Turnover)

d %>% gg_subseries(Turnover)

Explanation

Seasonal plot (gg_season):

  • Each coloured line is one year.
  • The peak occurs in December–January (Australian summer holiday period), and the trough in February.
  • The year-on-year increase is visible — later years (lighter colours) sit above earlier years throughout.

Subseries plot (gg_subseries):

  • Each panel shows one calendar month across all years; the blue horizontal line is the monthly mean.
  • Confirms that December and January have the highest average turnover; February the lowest.
  • The upward slope within every panel shows the year-on-year growth trend is consistent across all months.

Why is February low? Partly because February has fewer days — this motivates the calendar adjustment in the next part.

Part 3 — Calendar Adjustment

Make a calendar adjustment and re-plot the seasonal plots. Discuss whether there is a calendar effect.

Solution

d <- d %>%
  mutate(Days            = days_in_month(Month),
         Turnover_Per_Day = Turnover / Days)

d %>% gg_season(Turnover_Per_Day)

d %>% gg_subseries(Turnover_Per_Day)

Explanation

Variable Meaning
days_in_month(Month) Number of days in each month (28/29/30/31)
Turnover / Days Average daily turnover — removes the “more days = more sales” effect

Is there a calendar effect? Yes.

  • In the subseries plot, February’s mean rises relative to March once we divide by days, closing most of the gap with the surrounding months.
  • April, September, and November (30-day months) also shift slightly upward relative to the 31-day months.
  • This confirms that part of February’s apparent low turnover was simply due to it having fewer days, not genuinely lower daily trading activity.

Part 4 — Box-Cox Transformation

Perform a Box-Cox transformation on the calendar-adjusted series. What is the optimal \(\lambda\)?

Solution

lambda <- d %>%
  features(Turnover_Per_Day, features = guerrero) %>%
  pull(lambda_guerrero)
lambda
## [1] 0.2866775
d %>%
  autoplot(box_cox(Turnover_Per_Day, lambda)) +
  labs(title    = sprintf("Box-Cox transformed turnover (λ = %.4f)", lambda),
       y = "Transformed Turnover Per Day", x = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

Explanation

Box-Cox family:

\[ w_t = \begin{cases} \log(y_t) & \lambda = 0 \\ \dfrac{y_t^{\lambda} - 1}{\lambda} & \lambda \neq 0 \end{cases} \]

  • The Guerrero method selects \(\lambda\) to minimise the coefficient of variation of the subseries means — i.e., it looks for the \(\lambda\) that makes the seasonal variation most stable across the level of the series.
  • For this Queensland takeaway series the optimal \(\lambda \approx 0.29\), which is close to (but not exactly) a log transform (\(\lambda = 0\)).
  • The transformed series shows more stable variance across time, which is the goal: multiplicative seasonality becomes approximately additive after transformation.

Practical tip: Values of \(\lambda\) close to 0 are often rounded to 0 (log) for interpretability. Here, \(\lambda \approx 0.29\) is close enough that a log transform would also be reasonable.

Problem 2 — White Noise

Setup

set.seed(12345)
year <- 1900:2024
T    <- length(year)
data <- tsibble(year = year,
                y    = rnorm(T),
                index = year)

Note: The solutions file adds set.seed(12345) so that results are reproducible. The student version omits this, so individual plots will differ — but the qualitative conclusions should be the same.

Part 1 — Time Plot

Plot a time plot and comment on any features you observe.

Solution

data %>%
  autoplot(y) +
  labs(title = "White Noise Time Series (mean = 0, sd = 1)",
       y = "y", x = "Year") +
  theme_minimal()

Explanation

Feature Observation
Trend None — the series fluctuates around zero with no systematic direction
Seasonality None — the data are annual so intra-year seasonality cannot exist
Cycles None apparent
Variance Roughly constant (homoscedastic)
Range Approximately \([-2.5, 2.5]\), consistent with a standard Normal

This is expected: the series was generated as i.i.d. \(\mathcal{N}(0,1)\), so by construction there is no temporal structure.

Part 2 — ACF Plot with Custom Significance Lines

Plot the ACF, adding 90% significance lines in red. Hint: if qnorm(0.975) = 1.96, what is the new multiplier?

Solution

data %>%
  ACF(y) %>%
  autoplot() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-1, 1) * qnorm(0.95) / sqrt(T),
             colour = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "ACF of White Noise — blue = 95%, red = 90% significance") +
  theme_minimal()

Explanation

Where do the significance lines come from?

Under the null hypothesis that all autocorrelations are zero, each sample autocorrelation \(\hat{\rho}_k\) is approximately \(\mathcal{N}(0, 1/T)\) for large \(T\). A two-sided \(100(1-\alpha)\%\) significance band is therefore:

\[ \pm \frac{z_{1-\alpha/2}}{\sqrt{T}} \]

Level \(\alpha\) \(z_{1-\alpha/2}\) R expression
95% (default blue) 0.05 qnorm(0.975) \(\approx 1.96\) 1.96 / sqrt(T)
90% (red) 0.10 qnorm(0.95) \(\approx 1.645\) qnorm(0.95) / sqrt(T)

What to expect: For a white noise series of length \(T = 125\), we expect approximately 5% of the 95%-level bars to exceed the blue lines by chance alone, and 10% to exceed the narrower red lines.

Part 3 — Lag Plot

Produce a lag plot. Comment on why there is no colour. Comment on any patterns.

Solution

data %>%
  gg_lag(y, geom = "point", alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Lag plot of White Noise") +
  theme_minimal()

Explanation

Why no colour?

In fpp3, gg_lag() colours points by the season of the observation (e.g., month or quarter). This series has an annual index — there is only one observation per year and no sub-annual seasonality — so every observation belongs to the same “season” and a single colour is used throughout.

What patterns do we observe?

  • There is no discernible linear relationship between \(y_t\) and \(y_{t-k}\) at any of the displayed lags.
  • The point clouds are roughly circular (or formless), indicating near-zero autocorrelation at every lag.
  • This is exactly what we expect from a white noise process: successive values are independent, so knowing \(y_{t-k}\) tells us nothing about \(y_t\).
